kautilya.title: Kauṭilya's desk — the Arthaśāstra as counter-intelligence manual
kautilya.intro: The Arthaśāstra (c. Mauryan core, redacted by the early centuries CE) contains a complete intelligence doctrine: agent networks, integrity testing, disinformation, subversion, counter-espionage. The information war over Sanātana Dharma is being fought with techniques its own tradition codified two thousand years ago — so use its own countermeasures.

items.name: Upadhā — test before trust
items.ancient: Kauṭilya vetted ministers with staged integrity tests (the dharma, artha, kāma and bhaya upadhās) — loyalty was never assessed from words alone.
items.modern: Apply upadhā to content: the source test (who is this account, what else do they post?), the evidence test (can you reach a primary source in two minutes?), the incentive test (does the poster profit from your outrage?), the fear test (is it engineered to scare you into sharing?).
items.drill: Run ONE upadhā before amplifying. If the primary source can't be found in two minutes, the post has failed the test.

items.name: Gūḍhapuruṣa — assume organized actors
items.ancient: Entire chapters on covert agents run under cover identities — wandering ascetics, merchants, students — with separate handlers and cover stories. Kauṭilya assumes the information space is contested by professionals, not amateurs.
items.modern: So do modern platforms: 'coordinated inauthentic behavior' is standard doctrine. The 'random account' posting identical 'ancient Pakistan' templates across handles is a network, not a person — the NCRI's network analyses trace exactly these structures.
items.drill: Profile the account, not just the post: creation date, follower symmetry, posting cadence, template reuse across accounts.

items.name: The three-report rule — convergence before belief
items.ancient: Intelligence was accepted only when three INDEPENDENT agents' reports converged; agents who diverged were re-tasked or investigated. Independence, then convergence, then belief.
items.modern: The same doctrine as the NATO Admiralty code and tathya's own rule (flagship claims: ≥5 independent sources). A screenshot quoting a screenshot quoting a blog is one source wearing three coats.
items.drill: Count truly independent lines of evidence. One is an anecdote, two is a lead, three converging is a fact you can repeat.

items.name: Bheda — division is the cheapest weapon
items.ancient: Kauṭilya's manual for breaking enemy confederacies: agents plant stories that set allies against each other. The weapon is a narrative, not a sword — and it is listed as the CHEAPEST of the four upāyas (sāma, dāna, bheda, daṇḍa).
items.modern: Wedge content — caste vs caste, sect vs sect, Hindu vs Sikh, north vs south — that surges precisely when unity trends is bheda at platform scale. The rage you feel at your neighbor is the payload detonating.
items.drill: When a post makes you furious at a neighboring community, pause: who profits from this split? Check what else the account posts.

items.name: Upajāpa — demoralization warfare
items.ancient: Before a siege, agents and astrologers seed the enemy population with omens and despair — the fortress is softened by narrative before any weapon is raised.
items.modern: 'Your civilization is fake / stolen / backward / invented by the British' is upajāpa at scale: the target is morale, not truth. The counter is not louder shouting — it is documented pride: receipts, provenance, the atlas.
items.drill: Counter demoralization with one sourced fact, not ten angry replies. Calm receipts outperform outrage in every audience that matters.

analyst.title: The analyst's desk — declassified structured tradecraft
analyst.intro: The CIA publishes its analytic tradecraft precisely because smart people fool themselves in predictable ways. These are the techniques professionals use to keep their own biases out of the verdict — use them on every claim, including the ones you want to be true.

items.name: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH)
items.source: Heuer, Psychology of Intelligence Analysis (CIA Center for the Study of Intelligence, 1999), ch. 8
items.how: List the hypotheses FIRST, then score each piece of evidence against ALL of them. The winner is the hypothesis with the LEAST inconsistent evidence — not the most support. This inverts confirmation bias: you try to refute, not to confirm. Run the live matrix below on the Indo-Aryan question.
items.drill: If your matrix shows one hypothesis with zero inconsistencies on a contested question, you filled it in wrong.

items.name: Source grading — the Admiralty code
items.how: Grade the SOURCE (A–F reliability) and the CLAIM (1–6 credibility) separately — a reliable source can carry an unconfirmed claim, and an unknown account can post a true one. Fusing the two axes is how good sources launder bad claims. Try the grader below.
items.drill: Never say 'true/false' about a post again — say 'B2' or 'F5' and watch your own thinking sharpen.

items.name: SIFT — lateral reading
items.source: Caulfield (2019); Wineburg & McGrew, Stanford History Education Group (2017)
items.how: Stop. Investigate the source. Find better coverage. Trace to the original. Professional fact-checkers read ACROSS the web about a source rather than down its page — Stanford's studies showed they beat PhD historians at spotting fakes this way.
items.drill: Open three tabs about the SOURCE before reading the post twice. Two minutes, every time.

items.name: ABCDE — analyze the campaign, not the claim
items.source: Pamment, The EU's Role in Fighting Disinformation (2020); standard disinfo-analysis framing
items.how: Actor, Behavior, Content, Degree, Effect. A single false post is noise; the same template across fifty accounts with synchronized timing is an operation. tathya's flag form captures exactly these fields — channel, account pattern, variant text, spread — so flags aggregate into campaign pictures.
items.drill: Before rebutting content, note the behavior: how many accounts, what cadence, which audience? Sometimes the right response is documentation, not debate.

items.name: Key assumptions check
items.source: CIA, A Tradecraft Primer: Structured Analytic Techniques (2009)
items.how: List what must be true for the claim to hold, then attack each assumption separately. 'Taxila was a university' assumes institutional structure — one assumption, checkable, false. Most propaganda dies at its weakest hidden assumption.
items.drill: Write the claim's hidden assumptions as bullet points. Attack the weakest one first — it usually takes the whole post with it.

achDemo.question: Who were the Indo-Aryan speakers — migrants or indigenous? (the ledger's category-12 flagship)
evidence: Narasimhan et al. 2019 (Science): Steppe-pastoralist-related ancestry appears in South Asia in the 2nd millennium BCE
Shinde et al. 2019 (Cell): the Rakhigarhi Indus-city genome shows no Steppe ancestry
Indus-periphery ancestry remains the largest single component of most modern South Asians
Philology places Indo-Aryan inside the Indo-European family, with its closest kin (Iranian) outside India
The Ṛgveda's geography centers on the Punjab and names no homeland elsewhere
Mature Harappan strata have yielded no uncontested horse or spoked-chariot remains
